county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1891 from Colusa County. Named for Dr. Hugh J. Glenn, a wheat rancher. Agricultural county in the Sacramento Valley. Pre-1891 records are in Colusa County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Glenn County Recorder in Willows has deeds from 1891. Pre-1891 records are in Colusa County. Named for Dr. Hugh J. Glenn, one of the largest wheat growers in the world, who was murdered in 1883. Glenn County Museum preserves agricultural heritage records. Sacramento Valley agricultural records supplement genealogical research. FamilySearch has microfilmed some county records. Check the Glenn Transcript newspaper for obituaries and legal notices. The county's small population means families are traceable through land records, school censuses, and Great Register voter rolls from the 1890s.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Willows — index and images vary by era

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Annual property tax assessments from 1891. Valuable for tracking land ownership, crop types, and agricultural improvements in the San Joaquin/Sacramento Valley.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1853 from Trinity County. Named for Alexander von Humboldt. Eureka is the county seat and major lumber port. Wiyot, Yurok, Hoopa, and Karuk tribal lands. 1860 Wiyot Massacre at Indian Island is a significant historical event.
county_metadata.research_tips: Humboldt County Recorder in Eureka has deeds from 1853. The Humboldt County Historical Society in Eureka has extensive records of the lumber industry and maritime community. Clarke Historical Museum holds Native American artifacts and community records. Humboldt State University Library Special Collections has local history manuscripts. The 1860 Indian Island Massacre records are at Humboldt County Courthouse. Tribal records for Wiyot, Yurok, Hoopa, and Karuk are held by respective tribal governments. Check the Humboldt Times newspaper (from 1854) for early families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Eureka — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1907 from San Diego County. California's youngest county. Irrigation transformed the Colorado Desert into farmland after 1901. Large Mexican-American population with cross-border family ties to Mexicali.
county_metadata.research_tips: Imperial County Recorder in El Centro has records from 1907, making it California's youngest county. Pre-1907 records are in San Diego County. Cross-border research with Mexicali, Mexico is essential — many families have connections on both sides. Imperial Valley was transformed by irrigation after 1901. The Imperial Valley Pioneers Museum has early settler records. Check NARA for immigration and border crossing records at Calexico port of entry. FamilySearch has some microfilmed records. The Imperial Valley Press newspaper (from 1901) traces early settlement families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, El Centro — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1866 from Mono and Tulare counties. Contains Mount Whitney (highest point in contiguous US) and Death Valley (lowest point). Manzanar Japanese internment camp operated 1942-1945. Owens Valley water wars with Los Angeles.
county_metadata.research_tips: Inyo County Recorder in Independence has deeds from 1866. The Eastern California Museum in Independence holds Manzanar Japanese internment camp records and Owens Valley history. Manzanar National Historic Site archives document the 10,000+ Japanese Americans interned 1942-1945. Owens Valley water wars with Los Angeles (early 1900s) generated extensive legal records and depositions. Pre-1866 records split between Mono and Tulare counties. Death Valley mining records trace borax mining families. Check BLM land patent records for homesteaders and miners.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Independence — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1866 from Tulare and Los Angeles counties. Oil discovered in 1899 transformed the county. Bakersfield became a hub for Dust Bowl migrants from Oklahoma and Arkansas. Buck Owens and Merle Haggard defined the Bakersfield Sound.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kern County Recorder in Bakersfield has deeds from 1866. The Kern County Museum and Library hold extensive local history collections. Oil industry records from 1899 onward trace many families. Dust Bowl migrants from Oklahoma, Arkansas, and Texas settled in Kern County during the 1930s — Works Progress Administration records and Farm Security Administration photographs document them. Bakersfield Californian newspaper provides obituaries. Pre-1866 records split between Tulare and Los Angeles counties. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Taft and Maricopa for oil worker families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Bakersfield — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Tulare County. Agricultural county in the San Joaquin Valley. Hanford had a notable Chinatown (Taoist Temple still stands). Pre-1893 records are in Tulare County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kings County Recorder in Hanford has deeds from 1893. Pre-1893 records are in Tulare County. The Hanford Carnegie Museum preserves local history. The Taoist Temple in Hanford's China Alley reflects the significant Chinese community in the agricultural region. Japanese farming families were displaced during WWII internment. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. Check the Hanford Sentinel newspaper for obituaries and community notices. Agricultural census records supplement federal census for tracing farm families. Kings County is well-documented through county school district records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Hanford — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1861 from Napa County. Named for Clear Lake, California's largest natural freshwater lake. Pomo tribal homeland. Resort and agricultural community. Sulphur Bank Mine is now a Superfund site.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lake County Recorder in Lakeport has deeds from 1861. Pre-1861 records are in Napa County. Lake County Historical Society preserves local records and photographs. Clear Lake region has Pomo tribal heritage — check NARA BIA records for Round Valley and Middletown Rancheria. The county's resort history means seasonal populations are harder to trace. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. Check the Lake County Bee newspaper for obituaries and legal notices. Sulphur Bank Mine (now Superfund site) records trace mining families. Borax Lake archaeological site is one of California's oldest.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Lakeport — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 from Plumas and Shasta counties. Named for Danish immigrant Peter Lassen, a Gold Rush trail guide. Susanville named for Lassen's daughter. Logging and ranching economy. Tule Lake Japanese internment camp was partly in Lassen County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lassen County Recorder in Susanville has deeds from 1864. Named for Peter Lassen, a Danish immigrant and Gold Rush trail guide murdered in 1859. Lassen County Historical Society preserves ranching and logging community records. Tule Lake Japanese internment camp (Newell) was partly in Lassen County — check NARA for camp records. Pre-1864 records split between Plumas and Shasta counties. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. The Lassen Advocate newspaper provides obituaries. Check BLM land patents for homesteaders in this remote northeastern California county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Susanville — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Founded as a Spanish pueblo in 1781. Most populous county in the US. Spanish mission records from San Gabriel (1771) and San Fernando (1797). Mexican-era ranchos are key land records. Hollywood, entertainment industry archives.
county_metadata.research_tips: Los Angeles County Recorder has deed records from 1850, with some Spanish and Mexican-era land records preceding statehood. The Huntington Library in San Marino has premier manuscript collections. Los Angeles Public Library's History Department has city directories from 1872. The Archdiocese of Los Angeles Archives hold mission records from San Gabriel (1771) and San Fernando (1797). LAPL's Shades of L.A. photograph collection documents diverse communities. Seaver Center at the Natural History Museum has genealogical resources. Mexican-era rancho grants are essential for early families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Fresno County. Named for the Spanish word for lumber. Agricultural and timber county. Pre-1893 records in Fresno County. Significant Japanese farming communities before WWII internment.
county_metadata.research_tips: Madera County Recorder has deeds from 1893. Pre-1893 records are in Fresno County. Named for the Spanish word for lumber, reflecting the timber industry's importance. Madera County Museum preserves local history. Japanese farming families were prominent before WWII internment. Madera County Library has local history holdings. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. Check the Madera Tribune newspaper for obituaries and community notices. Central Valley agricultural records supplement federal census. Yosemite Lumber Company records trace timber industry families.
